summary: The author placed 4th by combining XGBoost and logistic regression to predict win/loss outcomes on point differential. They engineered Laplace-smoothed temporal features and stacked out-of-fold predictions on top of an existing feature set. Validation relied on a leave-one-season-out strategy guided by Brier score intuition rather than standard cross-validation, which suffered from time-based leakage. The final submission used model averaging across seasons to produce calibrated probabilities.
extensive_summary:
## Approach Overview
The solution targets the March Machine Learning Mania 2025 competition by predicting win/loss outcomes using a two-stage modeling pipeline. The core architecture applies logistic regression on the leaf nodes of an XGBoost model trained with a Cauchy loss function on point differential.

## Feature Engineering & Data
The author built upon the feature set from Raddar's Vilnius NCAA solution, adding Laplace-smoothed features derived from prior season matchups, away wins, and a 14-day win ratio. Out-of-fold predictions were uniquely stacked to enhance predictive power. Only Kaggle-published data was used, with no external data or manual manipulation.

## Validation & Leakage Mitigation
A major focus was avoiding temporal leakage. The author explicitly discarded the original CV code due to time-based leakage and removed a spline component that caused target leakage. Instead of traditional cross-validation, they employed a leave-one-season-out strategy for model averaging, guided by personal intuition calibrated against the Brier score of a left-out 2024 season. Models with superior but leaky local CV scores were intentionally rejected.

## Ensembling & Submission
The final submission leveraged model averaging across seasons. The author also notes a secondary submission utilizing Raddar's historical R-based GitHub solution. Reproducibility code is provided, though non-seeded team matches are hardcoded to 0.5 to save memory and compute time.
competition_flow: Raw Kaggle data is processed to extract temporal and matchup features, which are fed into an XGBoost model with Cauchy loss to generate leaf node predictions; these are combined via logistic regression and averaged across leave-one-season-out folds to produce final win/loss probabilities.
data_processing: Laplace smoothing on temporal features (prior season matchup, away wins, last 14-day win ratio)
Removing spline components to prevent target leakage
Hardcoding non-seeded team matches to 0.5 for memory efficiency
features_engineering: Laplace-smoothed features (prior season matchup, away wins, last 14-day win ratio)
Features inherited from Raddar's Vilnius NCAA solution
cv_strategy: Leave-one-season-out for model averaging, guided by Brier score intuition on a left-out 2024 season; explicitly avoided standard CV due to time-based leakage.
ensembling: Model averaging across leave-one-season-out predictions to produce final win/loss probabilities.
insights: Temporal leakage severely compromises validation in time-series sports data, making leave-one-season-out a more reliable averaging strategy than standard CV.
Brier score intuition on a held-out season effectively guides validation strategy selection.
Stacking out-of-fold predictions alongside Laplace-smoothed temporal features significantly boosts predictive performance.
critical_findings: Standard cross-validation code contained time-based leakage that artificially inflated local scores.
Including a spline component introduced target leakage, degrading model validity.
Models with 'better' leaky local CV scores were actually inferior and were correctly discarded.
what_did_not_work: Models with superior but leaky local CV scores
